---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex  # or xelatex
    # mathfont: "Latin Modern Math"
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]

---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\ \vspace{1em} \emph{09 - Test Selection for an Assessment}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\hfill \includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
}
\maketitle
```
:::

## Test Selection

Two moments of test selection:

1. Selection of tests to include in your own "toolbox"

2. **Selection of tests to use with a specific individual for a specific purpose.**

## Test Selection for a Specific Assessment

For clinical neuropsychology there are three main approaches (called in many ways in the literature)

- Fixed battery
- Flexible battery
- Fixed screening battery plus ad hoc tests

## Fixed Battery Approach

The test battery is the same for all evaluated individuals. It is generally quite extensive and tends to cover all cognitive domains.

It is very common especially in clinical settings where evaluation tends to be standardized for all patients and in contexts where the evaluated individuals tend to be quite similar.

## Fixed Battery Approach

**PROS**

- It is an approach that is not influenced by the evaluator's preconceptions.
- It allows a comprehensive evaluation of the patient.

**CONS**

- It requires a lot of time (often multiple sessions) to complete a single evaluation.
- It can be very fatiguing for the person administering the tests.

## Flexible Battery Approach

Tests are chosen each time based on diagnostic hypotheses about the examinee. It is accompanied by the so-called hypothesis testing process.

_NOTE: in forensic settings, this is the most common approach, also due to limited evaluation time_


## Flexible Battery Approach

In general one starts with a test, selected based on diagnostic questions. Based on the result of the test, the next test is selected and so on.

Often a favored approach is to seek to falsify (that is, refute) one's hypotheses, rather than confirm them, following a deductive approach.

E.g. if I suspect that the patient has a memory deficit and I find that the memory test is within normal range, I have falsified my hypothesis and can proceed with another hypothesis to investigate through another test.

## Flexible Battery Approach

**PROS**

- It is an approach that allows focus on the specific diagnostic requirements.
- The duration is limited.

**CONS**

- The outcome can be influenced by the neuropsychologist's preconceptions (e.g., confirmation bias).
- Crucial elements of the patient's cognitive status could be overlooked.
- An approach strictly based on falsification does not account for possible measurement errors.

## Approach with Fixed Screening Battery Plus Ad Hoc Tests

It is an intermediate approach to the two. One begins with a summary evaluation done with a screening battery, followed by an in-depth evaluation based on the screening results.

**PROS** and **CONS** are intermediate between the two previous approaches.


## Other Considerations on Test Selection

An important consideration to make about test selection is that you must remember that (from a statistical point of view), the more tests you administer, the more likely it is that at least one will fall below the cutoff.

One possibility would be to correct the tests with multiple comparison correction methods (e.g., Bonferroni). In the Bonferroni method, to keep the type 1 error at the nominal level (alpha = 0.05, i.e., 5%) for all tests, you adjust the critical value by dividing by the number of tests administered.

Suppose we administered 20 tests, the critical alpha would become 0.05/20 = 0.0025

## Other Considerations on Test Selection

Using corrections like the Bonferroni method has another problem: it assumes that all test results are independent. We know instead that, coming from the same individual, they will likely be related.

There are therefore more correct methods that increase type 2 error less, in fact not completely controlling the 1st type. E.g., Benjamini & Yekutieli 2001, False Discovery Rate.

_The use of test correction methods is anyway very uncommon and could only be used with those methods that return exact probability._
